result.meeting_context: The Goal Setting Subcommittee met to review and direct the Fiscal Years 2023–24 and 2024–25 Council work plan development process. Members discussed timeline, analysis criteria, advisory body participation, and prioritization categories, approved prior minutes, and provided guidance to staff on process improvements, transparency, and equity considerations. Public comment addressed meeting access and hybrid participation preferences.

points.speaker: Laurel James
points.text: Staff presented the proposed work plan development process and sought subcommittee feedback on analysis criteria, advisory body input, and a prioritization approach. The process begins in January and culminates in June 2023 adoption alongside the FY 2023/24 budget, with regular check‑ins during implementation.
points.source: Laurel James : So the purpose of this presentation, as you mentioned, is to provide an overview of the proposed work plan development process...and an approach to categorize projects by priority.

points.speaker: Audrey Ramberg
points.text: Next steps are to incorporate subcommittee input and bring the development process to Council on January 24 for adoption, followed by a February 28 update on the FY21–23 work plan (with the midyear budget) and start of FY23–25 project discussion.
points.source: Audrey Ramberg : Following this meeting will be for staff to incorporate the Subcommittee's input...to bring to the Council in January on the 24th...followed by a February 28th update...and also the beginning of the fiscal year 2325 work plan project discussion.

points.speaker: Kimbre
points.text: Staff emphasized biannual progress check‑ins throughout the two‑year cycle to improve transparency and accountability, noting visuals and timelines can be included in the Council agenda report to clearly show how the process will unfold.
points.source: Kimbre : ...check insurance, which I know that that's been important to council, which essentially is a check in every six months over the full two years. We can certainly include those sort of visual aids and charts in the actual agenda staff report.

points.speaker: Laurel James
points.text: Proposed analysis will consider impact on strategic priorities, equity implications for underserved communities, dependencies and future capacity impacts, staff capacity, and costs and revenues. Staff requested input on any additional criteria the subcommittee wants included.
points.source: Laurel James : One of the items we're seeking feedback on today is analysis criteria...impact on achieving the strategic priorities...equity...impact on future capacity...Staff capacity...and then finally, staff will estimate the cost...

points.speaker: Ellen Kamei
points.text: Ellen Kamei requested that equity analysis align with the City’s Race Equity and Inclusion Action Plan and that each proposed project explicitly indicate which of the seven strategic priorities it advances to reinforce consistency and strategic alignment.
points.source: Ellen Kamei : ...under the umbrella of equity...we have our race equity and inclusion action plan...is there going to be like analysis...And then another item...is to include the alignment on the strategic priorities. We have 7 strategic priorities...I'd love to see...alignment...

points.speaker: Alison Hicks
points.text: Alison Hicks asked staff to add urgency or timeliness as a criterion so projects with time‑sensitive risks (e.g., Moffett Precise Plan impacts on neighborhood‑serving retail) can be appropriately prioritized earlier in the cycle.
points.source: Alison Hicks : ...the one thing that I sort of see missing...is if there's a sense of urgency to any of the particular items...one that comes to mind for me is the Moffett Precise plan...there's a sense of urgency...

points.speaker: Laurel James
points.text: Staff proposed having City Manager’s Office staff and advisory body liaisons attend meetings to facilitate structured work plan discussions, gather high‑level input on projects and priorities, identify critical issues, and summarize feedback for Council’s prioritization.
points.source: Laurel James : The proposed process is that CMO staff...and Council Advisory Body Staff liaisons will attend regular meetings to facilitate a structured discussion...eliciting feedback on high level input...identify critical issues...and...share input...The feedback will be summarized and provided to Council...

points.speaker: Ellen Kamei
points.text: The subcommittee asked for a consistent, equitable solicitation method across all advisory bodies using one or two standardized questions, enabling each body to provide broad feedback and feel heard, while ensuring capacity constraints are clearly communicated.
points.source: Ellen Kamei : ...narrowing the scope...Perhaps one or two questions for each advisory body would be a way to to make it more equitable...and then being able to provide that back to council...

points.speaker: Audrey Ramberg
points.text: Staff clarified advisory bodies cannot provide input before Council’s February identification of projects due to timing. Miss James will attend each advisory meeting to ensure consistent explanations of process and questions across bodies.
points.source: Audrey Ramberg : ...we wouldn't be able to do that in advance of your February identification of projects...our office which is namely Miss James would be going to each of the advisory body meetings...to present...and provide that consistency...

points.speaker: Laurel James
points.text: To address capacity limits, staff proposed three priority buckets: A (highest, first in allocation), B (next), and C (important but executed as time/resources allow). This structure introduces hierarchy to manage a large work plan within finite resources.
points.source: Laurel James : ...proposing that Council prioritize projects based on buckets...A would be the highest priority...B would be high priority...and then projects...in the C bucket...worked on as time and resources allow...to address the workload and capacity considerations.

points.speaker: Kimbre
points.text: Staff will bring an initial A/B/C categorization based on analysis for Council to adjust, avoiding a 1–50 ranking or dot exercises. To keep the plan realistic, staff noted a target around 40 items and cautioned against 80–90 item work plans.
points.source: Kimbre : ...staff would suggest the three buckets...give you all something to react to...we were definitely trying to avoid...one through 50...getting to a work plan that has, you know, 40 items on it is probably most realistic...we just can't have like the work plans of the past...

points.speaker: Ellen Kamei
points.text: Ellen Kamei requested clearer status transparency by showing each project’s estimated completion quarter/year and its ABC priority, noting at least 33 items are currently in progress; in‑progress items would generally be treated as category A to finish them.
points.source: Ellen Kamei : ...there were at least 33 in progress items...I'd love just a separate column that tells me maybe like projected date completed Q3 and then the update...and...another column that said priority A...

points.speaker: Kimbre
points.text: Staff agreed most in‑progress projects should be completed in this cycle and will clarify timelines and status in future updates; items already underway would generally be Category A, while not‑started items may be reconsidered or removed.
points.source: Kimbre : I think most of the projects that are in progress should be completed in this work plan cycle...we would put those items that have already been started probably in category A...The items that are not yet started...would really be the question...to...continue...or...remove...

points.speaker: Kimbre
points.text: Housing Element implementation items are non‑discretionary over the eight‑year cycle. Council can prioritize which to tackle in the next two years, but staff must balance choices with available capacity—multiple items cannot be done simultaneously.
points.source: Kimbre : ...all of those items that are going to end up being included in the housing element are non discretionary...we do have to do them within the next eight-year housing element cycle...you all will have the opportunity to discuss that...staff's going to also have to look at just their capacity...

points.speaker: Laurel James
points.text: Staff clarified that the tabled attachment was a recent status update on the FY21–23 work plan, and the non‑discretionary list includes Housing Element actions scheduled within this work plan; later‑scheduled items were not included.
points.source: Laurel James : ...that attachment is the most recent update on the current existing work plan...the non discretionary projects list...includes the housing element projects...scheduled for during this work plan...projects...further out...were not listed...

points.speaker: Lucas Ramirez
points.text: Lucas Ramirez urged presenting the full workload—core services, non‑discretionary obligations, and ongoing programmatic plans—alongside council‑initiated ideas so the Council prioritizes within realistic capacity and avoids repeatedly rolling over older priorities.
points.source: Lucas Ramirez : ...we should no longer divorce counsel initiated projects from the rest of the immense workload...share with us what that work looks like...so...we all come in with similar expectations that what's achievable...

points.speaker: Ellen Kamei
points.text: To support holistic visibility, Ellen Kamei suggested consistent, side‑by‑side tables: one listing the full work plan (with department lead, status, and origin) and another listing goal‑setting proposals, presented visually to aid council deliberations.
points.source: Ellen Kamei : ...would it be possible to get them...visually...attachment to have a work plan that's a table and then for attachment 3 have a work plan of our goal setting...and maybe we can add a a section in the table that kind of clarifies...the genesis of the item.

topics.topic: Public access and hybrid participation feedback

points.speaker: Bruce England
points.text: A public commenter asked that the published Zoom link include the meeting ID and not require manual email entry, noting accessibility issues on mobile devices. He also urged the City to prioritize hybrid meeting capability for inclusion, even if February in‑person plans are delayed.
points.source: Bruce England : ...the link to the Zoom meeting does not include the meeting ID...and it also asks for the person's e-mail address...not user friendly...the goal...should be to always include the meeting ID...and to not require somebody entering their e-mail address...really against [in-person only] just for the sake of inclusion and equity...

points.speaker: Bruce England
points.text: The commenter also suggested considering an International Dark Sky ordinance, clarifying the intranet redesign’s scope, updating status entries, consolidating tree and biodiversity items, treating the legislative program as ongoing, and addressing hybrid meetings under COVID‑19 recovery efforts.
points.source: Bruce England : ...potential projects previously raised by council members...International Dark Sky Association ordinance...intranet design redesign...a lot of the updates aren't actually up to date...why are four one and four five separate?...develop and implement and enhanced legislative program...oversee COVID-19 recovery efforts...

points.speaker: Lucas Ramirez
points.text: The subcommittee approved the December 15, 2020 meeting minutes unanimously. Alison Hicks moved to accept the minutes as written, Ellen Kamei seconded, and the roll‑call vote was 3‑0 in favor.
points.source: Laurel James : Committee member Hicks. / Alison Hicks : Yes. / Laurel James : Committee member Kumay. / Ellen Kamei : Yes / Laurel James : Chair Ramirez. / Lucas Ramirez : Yes, fantastic. ... Passes unanimously.

points.speaker: Lucas Ramirez
points.text: Ellen Kamei thanked staff for clarifying that none of the current members served on the prior goal setting subcommittee, and Lucas Ramirez adjourned the meeting at 5:35 PM with thanks to participants.
points.source: Lucas Ramirez : This meeting is adjourned at 5:35 PM...It has been a pleasure to chair this meeting for the first and last time for this committee. I should say thank you, everybody.

decisions.text: Place the January Council work plan process item on the consent calendar and include clear visuals/timelines in the staff report.
decisions.source: Lucas Ramirez : So we'll start with question one. I was outvoted, so this will be a consent item.

decisions.text: Incorporate additional analysis criteria: urgency/timeliness, explicit mapping to the seven strategic priorities, and equity analysis aligned with the Race Equity and Inclusion Action Plan.
decisions.source: Lucas Ramirez : For question 2...add a an urgency or timeliness criterion...have a criterion around alignment with the strategic priorities and then to to incorporate into the equity criterion the REI action plan...

decisions.text: Implement a standardized advisory body input process using the same broad questions across all bodies, with a CMO representative attending each meeting; allow bodies to provide broad feedback beyond narrow scopes.
decisions.source: Lucas Ramirez : For question 3, there's majority support for applying A consistent process across all of the council advisory bodies...the same questions...and...a representative from the city manager's office will be...to...solicit input.

decisions.text: Adopt three prioritization buckets (A, B, C) and have staff present an initial categorization for Council to adjust during prioritization.
decisions.source: Lucas Ramirez : For question 4 support for the categorization buckets that are proposed by staff and staff will produce the preliminary set of recommendations...

decisions.text: Enhance status reporting by showing each project’s estimated completion quarter/year and its ABC priority, and provide a process to remove stale or unnecessary items.
decisions.source: Lucas Ramirez : ...including the the quarter and year the estimated completion and then I think also including the relative priority ABC...and having some type of process for the removal of projects.

decisions.text: Approve the December 15, 2020 meeting minutes as written.
decisions.source: Lucas Ramirez : ...the motion is in order to approve these Minutes... / Alison Hicks : I move to accept the minutes as written. / Ellen Kamei : I'll second the motion. / ... Passes unanimously.
